segments.title: Calais, the BEF retreat, and the road to Dunkirk
segments.summary: Julian Thompson's account follows the pressure on Calais and shows why decisions about its defence mattered to the BEF's developing retreat toward Dunkirk.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends Julian Thompson's Dunkirk: Retreat to Victory for its maps, illustrations, and detail. The passage he reads traces the German armoured advance, the defence of Calais, and a petrol convoy ordered toward Dunkirk before the British high command had formally decided on the wider withdrawal. It makes the campaign's choices look less like a single prearranged evacuation and more like urgent local decisions made amid a rapidly narrowing route.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke introduces Thompson's book and reads its account of Calais, German panzer movements, and the convoy sent toward Dunkirk.

segments.title: Eyewitness accounts of the retreat
segments.summary: Forgotten Voices of Dunkirk uses first-hand testimony to bring out the confusion, exhaustion, congested roads, and destructive logistics of the retreat.
segments.body: Joshua Levine's collection is built around accounts from people who were there, rather than a single operational narrative. The excerpts move from hard-pressed infantry and a withdrawal through refugee-clogged roads to a petrol-transport corporal ordered to destroy aviation fuel before withdrawing. Together they show how the evacuation was experienced through improvised orders, threatened stores, and fragmented information at ground level.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke describes the oral-history collection and reads accounts from infantry officers and a Royal Army Service Corps corporal.

segments.title: Why the East Mole became an evacuation lifeline
segments.summary: Robert Jackson's account explains how air damage limited Dunkirk's harbour and made the narrow East Mole central to getting troops aboard ships.
segments.body: The main harbour's dock basins had been blocked by air attack, leaving only a small number of usable embarkation points. Dr. Clarke's excerpt highlights the East Mole's narrow dimensions and its survival under attack despite being an obvious target. This is a useful reminder that an evacuation depended on physical harbour infrastructure as much as on the famous image of boats waiting offshore.

evidence.note: The quoted passage describes the blocked harbour, remaining embarkation points, and the dimensions and resilience of the East Mole.

segments.title: The destroyer commitment behind the evacuation
segments.summary: Destroyers could ferry troops and defend the evacuation, but drawing them from fleet and convoy duties exposed wider Royal Navy priorities in 1940.
segments.body: The book passage presents destroyers as fast, manoeuvrable ships with valuable anti-aircraft firepower, even though they were poor substitutes for proper troop transports. It also frames their removal from other duties as a major risk just as Atlantic shipping protection was becoming critical. Dr. Clarke qualifies the claim that most Royal Navy destroyers were in the Far East, stressing instead the demands of Norway and early Atlantic convoy work.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke reads the assessment of destroyers at Dunkirk and comments on their actual global distribution and convoy role.

segments.title: RAF interception and the fuel problem over Dunkirk
segments.summary: Air Battle for Dunkirk shifts attention to RAF patrols, interception efforts, and the range limits that constrained fighters over the evacuation area.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that the RAF tried to intercept German aircraft before they reached Dunkirk, even when those efforts were not always visible to troops on the ground. The book's squadron accounts show patrols interrupted by engine trouble, fuel shortages, and brief contacts with German bombers. He links the problem to the wider geography of air power: fuel limits that helped Britain during the Battle of Britain constrained RAF fighters when they had to operate farther from their bases over Dunkirk.

evidence.note: Dr. Clarke introduces the RAF-focused book, reads several patrol accounts, and explains the importance of fuel range.
